blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْفَلَقِ (۱)
Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of the daybreak (1)
مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ (۲)
from the evil of what He has created, (2)
وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ (۳)
and from the evil of the dark night when it falls, (3)
وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّـٰثَـٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ (٤)
and from the evil of the witches, who blow on knots, (4)
وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ (٥)
and from the evil of the envious one when he envies.’ (5)

Key Points

The word falaq, like fajr, means splitting or cleaving. Splitting the darkness of the night or the dawn: "Fāliqu al-Iṣbāḥ" (The Cleaver of the Dawn) (263); splitting the seed within the earth. "Fāliqu al-Ḥabbi wa an-Nawā" (The Cleaver of the Grain and the Date-Stone) (264).

The meaning of ghāsiq is the beginning of the night, which brings darkness. As He says in another Ayah: "Aqimi aṣ-Ṣalāta li-dulūki ash-Shamsi ilā ghasaqi al-Layl" (Establish the prayer from the decline of the sun until the darkness of the night) (265). Perhaps the intent of ghāsiq is any darkness-bringing entity that brings and spreads spiritual darkness. Since wicked individuals use the darkness of night for attack, assault, or plotting, we therefore seek refuge in Allah from the evils that occur at night.

Naffāthāt is derived from nafth, meaning blowing, and ‘uqad is the plural of ‘aqd, meaning knot.

There is a possibility of evil arising in everything; even perfections like knowledge, benevolence, courage, and worship have afflictions. The affliction of knowledge is arrogance; the affliction of benevolence is reminding others of the favor (imposing obligation); the affliction of worship is vanity (‘ujb); and the affliction of courage is oppression and recklessness.

Three principles exist in vulnerability: 1. Readiness for attack by the enemy. 2. Suitable opportunities for attack, such as darkness. 3. The existence of gaps and weak points. Yes, the enemy seeks darkness, gaps, and weak points.

Malicious propaganda, creating negative atmospheres, tale-bearing, espionage, and spreading rumors are examples of Naffāthāt fī al-‘Uqad (those who blow upon the knots), meaning they blow to loosen firm bonds. Just as some people cause discord between spouses through temptation and doubt: "Mā yufarriqūna bayna al-mar’i wa zawjih" (They separate a man and his wife) (266).

Although everything Allah has created is good: "Alladhī aḥsana kulla shay’in khalaqah" (Who perfected everything He created) (267). However, if anything is not placed on its correct path, it becomes evil. Just as iron is good, but if it becomes a knife, there is the possibility of misuse. "Min sharri mā khalaq" (From the evil of what He created). "Sharri mā khalaq" means that evil originates from the creatures, not from the Creator, nor resulting from the act of creation itself.

Seeking refuge in Allah from dangers is not achieved merely by saying a‘ūdhu (I seek refuge); rather, action is necessary in addition to speech. Just as the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet علیهم السلام said, after feeding the needy, the orphan, and the captive: "Innā nakhāfu min Rabbinā yawman ‘abūsan qamṭarīrā" (Indeed, we fear from our Lord a Day of distress and gloom) (268). We fed them because we fear the Day of Judgment. The Quran says: "Fa waqāhumu Allāhu sharra dhālika al-yawm" (So Allah protected them from the evil of that Day) (269). Allah also saved them from the evil of that Day. Therefore, merely saying Innā nakhāfu is not effective; it must be accompanied by action.

A man with an unpleasant appearance complained to the Messenger of Allah about the ugliness of his face. Gabriel descended and conveyed Allah’s greeting to that man and said that Allah says: "Amā tarḍā an uḥsharta ‘alā jamāli Jibrīl?" (Are you not pleased that you will be resurrected with the beauty of Gabriel?) The man became joyful and decided to perform all obligatory and recommended acts of worship (270).

Absolute evil does not exist, and Allah has not created it. Rather, many things appear evil from one perspective but good from another. As Mawlawi (Rumi) says: The snake's venom is life for the snake, But for man, it is death. Thus, there is no absolute evil in the world; Evil is relative, know this too.

Some hardships and pressures are not evil; rather, they are the key to perfection. Mawlawi says: They cast a grain of wheat beneath the soil, Then from the soil, they built up its sheaves. Once again, they crushed it in the mill, Its value increased, and the bread became life-giving. Again, they crushed the bread beneath the teeth, It became beneficial intellect, soul, and understanding. Again, that soul, when it became absorbed in love, Became "Yu‘jibu az-Zurra‘" (pleasing to the sowers) after the harvest.

The Quran advises that you treat your wives kindly, and if you dislike them, perhaps there are hidden favors alongside that dislike. "Wa ‘āshirūhunna bi-al-ma‘rūfi fa-in karihtumūhunna fa-‘asā an takrahū shay’an wa yaj‘ala Allāhu fīhi khayran kathīrā" (And live with them in kindness. For if you dislike them, perhaps you dislike a thing and Allah makes therein much good) (271). Just as sometimes a woman is not beautiful, but she becomes the mother of great and distinguished children.

Many bitter experiences blossom our talents, cultivate the state of patience and resistance within us, and make the human heart humble and submissive. "La‘allahum yaḍḍarra‘ūn" (So that they might humble themselves) (272).

Night is a time for servitude and worship for the men of Allah: "Yatlūna āyāti Allāhi ānā’a al-layl" (They recite the verses of Allah during the hours of the night) (273). But for the ignoble, it is a time for plotting, threatening, and attacking. "Min sharri ghāsiqin" (From the evil of the darkness).

Any speech or action that destabilizes firm relationships and friendships is naffāthāt, and one must seek refuge in Allah from it.

Man's seeking refuge in Allah necessitates Allah granting refuge; otherwise, the command to seek refuge would be void.

Among all evils, the names of three evils are mentioned in this Surah: - Hidden evils and those in darkness. "Min sharri ghāsiqin idhā waqab" - Evils of impure tongues. "Min sharri an-Naffāthāti fī al-‘Uqad" - Evils of envy and negative rivalries. "Wa min sharri ḥāsidin idhā ḥasad"

Some women, due to their beauty and gentleness in speech, can weaken men in their decisions and be an instance of naffāthāt.

One instance of naffāthāt is sorcery and magic, and therefore learning and practicing it is forbidden (ḥarām).

Although "sharri mā khalaq" includes all evils, the names of three evils are mentioned separately due to their importance. "Ghāsiqin idhā waqab - an-Naffāthāti fī al-‘Uqad - ḥāsidin idhā ḥasad."

Seeking refuge is necessary in every state, whether there is danger: "sharri mā khalaq," or whether it is receiving perfection: "Fa-idhā qara’ta al-Qur’āna fa-ista‘idh bi-Allāhi mina ash-Shayṭāni ar-Rajīm" (So when you recite the Qur’an, seek refuge in Allah from Satan, the expelled) (274).

In Surah Al-Falaq, against three external evils, we seek refuge in one Divine attribute. (We sought refuge from the evil of ghāsiq, naffāthāt, and ḥāsid in the attribute of "Rabbi al-Falaq.") But in Surah An-Nas, the opposite is true. Against one internal danger, we seek refuge in three Divine attributes. (We seek refuge from the evil of the whispering of the heart in the attributes of "Rabbi an-Nās," "Maliki an-Nās," and "Ilāhi an-Nās.")

A Discussion on Envy

Striving to attain the perfections that others possess is ghibṭah (admiration/positive emulation) and is valuable, but striving to destroy the perfections of others is envy (ḥasādah).

Envy is a sign of low capacity and narrow-mindedness.

The first murder that occurred on earth was due to envy between the sons of Adam, and the first sin that occurred in heaven was the envy of Iblis toward Adam.

We read in a Hadith: Envy consumes faith just as fire consumes wood (275).

What befell the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet علیهم السلام when they were sidelined was due to envy. The Quran states in Ayah 54 of Surah An-Nisā’: Why do they envy worthy individuals because of Allah’s favors and grace? Do they not know that We gave the family of Abraham the Book, wisdom, and a great kingdom?

Envy is the most prominent evil among all evils, because the envious person plots, schemes, and commits all kinds of evils to reach his goal.

Imam Ali عليه السلام says: "Al-Ḥasadu dā’un ‘iyā’un lā yazālu illā bi-halaki al-ḥāsidi aw bi-mawti al-maḥsūd" (Envy is a dangerous disease that does not cease except with the destruction of the envious person or the death of the envied person) (276).

The envious person is essentially protesting against Allah and opposing His will and wisdom, questioning why He granted to that person and not to him.

The envious person never offers goodwill or advice, and due to the nature of envy, he prevents his own good from reaching others.

The Quran considers the root of many sins to be baghyan (transgression), meaning envy. Imam Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Inna al-kufra aṣluhu al-ḥasad" (Indeed, the root of disbelief is envy) (277). Just as they said in response to the Prophets: "A-basharun yahdūnanā?" (Shall a human being guide us?) (278). Should a man like us become a Prophet and guide us?

The envious person is not characterized by humility or gratitude, and he never testifies to the perfections of others, inevitably committing the sin of concealing the truth.

The envious person is usually a hypocrite. Imam Ali عليه السلام says: "Al-Ḥāsidu yuẓhiru wuddahu fī aqwālihi wa yukhfī bughḍahu fī af‘ālihi" (The envious person shows his affection in his words but conceals his hatred in his actions) (279).

What is the Source of Envy?

Sometimes inappropriate discrimination among individuals provokes their envy.

Sometimes people are not informed and assume conditions are equal, and as soon as they see a superiority or advantage, they become envious. Whereas if they knew the wisdom and reason behind the differences, they would become calm.

Envy also appears within the families of prophethood, just as in the house of Prophet Jacob, the envy of the sons compelled them to throw their brother into the well.

The scope of envy is not limited to material blessings; rather, sometimes people are envied for their faith, and others wish to turn them back to disbelief. "Yaruddūnakum min ba‘di īmānikum kuffāran ḥasadan" (They wish they could turn you back to disbelief after your belief, out of envy) (280).

It is clear that envy is the desire for the destruction of others' blessings; otherwise, requesting blessings from Allah is not envy. Just as Solomon asked Allah for an unparalleled kingdom: "Mulkan lā yanbaghī li-aḥad" (A kingdom that should not belong to anyone [after me]) (281). And Imam Ali عليه السلام asks Allah in the Kumayl Supplication that his share be greater and his proximity to Allah be greater: "Wa ij‘alnī min aḥsani ‘abīdika naṣīban ‘indaka wa aqrabihim manzila minnka..." (And make me among the best of Your servants in share with You and the closest of them in station to You...).

To treat envy, one must think like this:

The world is short and small, and worrying about it is not worthwhile. We also have blessings that others do not possess. Blessings are distributed based on wisdom, even if we do not know the reason for it. He whose blessings are greater has greater responsibility. We must know that our envy is fruitless, and Allah will not cut off His grace just because a servant cannot bear to see a blessing; we only torment ourselves.

263) Al-An‘ām, 96. 264) Al-An‘ām, 95. 265) Al-Isrā’, 78. 266) Al-Baqarah, 102. 267) As-Sajdah, 7. 268) Al-Insān, 10. 269) Al-Insān, 11. 270) Biḥār al-Anwār, Vol. 22, p. 140. 271) An-Nisā’, 19. 272) Al-A‘rāf, 94. 273) Āl ‘Imrān, 113. 274) An-Naḥl, 98. 275) Al-Kāfī, Vol. 2, p. 306. 276) Nahj al-Balāghah, Maxim 493. 277) Al-Kāfī, Vol. 8, p. 7. 278) At-Taghābun, 6. 279) Ghurar al-Ḥikam. 280) Al-Baqarah, 109. 281) Ṣād, 35.

Messages

  1. Seeking refuge in Allah must be expressed verbally. "Qul a‘ūdhu"
  2. Due to the multitude and importance of dangers, even the Prophets must seek refuge in Allah. "Qul a‘ūdhu"
  3. Self-reform and societal reform are impossible without seeking aid and refuge in Allah. "Qul a‘ūdhu bi-Rabbi al-Falaq"
  4. Since evils lock the human heart and mind, one must seek refuge in a Power that is the Cleaver and Breaker of locks and obstacles. "Bi-Rabbi al-Falaq"
  5. Approach supplication with a broad perspective. "Sharri mā khalaq" (It encompasses all creatures.)
  6. Among all evils, the evil of envy, sowing discord, breaking covenants, and hidden plots have greater importance. "Min sharri ghāsiqin... sharri an-Naffāthāt... sharri ḥāsid"
  7. The enemy blows until its propaganda becomes effective. (Naffāthāt is the plural of naffāthah, which is an intensive form.)
  8. Through whispers, beliefs are weakened, and words take effect. "Min sharri an-Naffāthāti fī al-‘Uqad"
  9. Envy has no cure except seeking refuge in Allah. "Qul a‘ūdhu... idhā ḥasad"
  10. Potential dangers become perilous when they materialize. "Ḥāsidin idhā ḥasad"
  11. Envy exists to some extent in individuals; acting upon it is a sin. "Idhā ḥasad"
